title: Writing - Part 2
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 140-190 words.

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt:
Your school's student welfare committee has asked you to write a report on a survey about students' stress levels and their satisfaction with the mental health support currently available. Include findings about the main sources of stress, describe gaps in current provision, and make recommendations for improvements.

Write your report.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Report on Student Stress Levels and Mental Health Support at Glenwood Academy

Introduction
This report presents findings from a survey of 220 students investigating stress levels, causes of anxiety, and satisfaction with mental health support provision.

Main Sources of Stress
The survey identified examination pressure as the most frequently cited source of stress, reported by 74% of respondents. Other significant factors included social media comparison (52%), concerns about future employment (48%), and difficulties managing workload across multiple subjects (41%). Alarmingly, 36% of students reported experiencing persistent feelings of anxiety or low mood during the current academic year.

Gaps in Current Support Provision
While the school employs two counsellors, the average waiting time for an appointment was reported as three weeks — a delay that students described as unacceptably long during periods of crisis. Many students were also unaware of the self-referral process or the external support services available to them.

Recommendations
The school should urgently increase its counselling capacity through additional staff or partnerships with external providers. A peer mentoring programme using trained older students would also be valuable. All students should receive clear information about available support at the start of each academic year.

tasks.title: Article
tasks.prompt:
You see this notice in an English-language magazine:

'Stressed and struggling? With mental health challenges affecting young people in record numbers, we want articles exploring practical ways to manage stress and improve mental wellbeing. Share your advice and experiences.'

Write your article.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Small Steps, Big Difference: Managing Stress in a Pressured World

If you are reading this article, there is a reasonable chance you are feeling overwhelmed. You are not alone. Mental health challenges among young people have reached levels that demand we take them seriously — and that we talk about practical solutions rather than offering empty reassurances.

The most consistently effective strategy I have discovered is deceptively simple: physical movement. Exercise does not require a gym membership or an hour of your time — even a twenty-minute walk can dramatically reduce cortisol levels and shift your perspective on whatever was troubling you.

Equally important is establishing genuine digital boundaries. Social media, used thoughtlessly, is a machine for generating anxiety and inadequacy. Scheduling screen-free periods each day — particularly before sleep — can have a transformative effect on both mood and sleep quality.

Learning to recognise and challenge negative thought patterns is perhaps the most powerful long-term tool. Cognitive behavioural techniques, accessible through books, apps, or therapy, teach you to question anxious thinking rather than simply accepting it as truth.

None of these solutions is magic. But each one, practised consistently, genuinely helps.

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt:
You have been asked to write a review for an English-language lifestyle website. Write a review of a book, podcast, or film related to mental health awareness that you have experienced recently. Describe what it covers, evaluate how useful or insightful it is, and say whether you would recommend it to others.

Write your review.
tasks.modelAnswer:
'The Anxiety Toolkit' Podcast — A Genuinely Useful Listen

In a field saturated with well-meaning but vague advice, 'The Anxiety Toolkit' stands out as a podcast that takes mental health seriously and treats its listeners as intelligent adults.

Hosted by a clinical psychologist and a former anxiety sufferer, each episode tackles a specific aspect of anxiety or stress management with a refreshing combination of scientific rigour and personal honesty. Topics covered in the series I listened to included perfectionism, social anxiety, sleep disturbance, and navigating the mental health system — all presented clearly and accessibly.

What distinguishes this podcast from many similar offerings is its emphasis on practical application. Each episode concludes with specific, evidence-based exercises that listeners can implement immediately. I found the episode on 'cognitive defusion' — a technique for distancing yourself from unhelpful thoughts — particularly transformative.

If I were to identify a weakness, it would be that some episodes assume a degree of prior familiarity with therapeutic concepts that genuine newcomers might find challenging.

Overall, however, I would enthusiastically recommend 'The Anxiety Toolkit' to anyone looking to better understand and manage their mental health. It is thoughtful, practical, and genuinely compassionate.
